behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The bug could live in two places: (1) the tool not passing rename-detection flags (e.g., -M or --diff-filter=R) to git, or (2) the parser not recognising 'R' status entries. A unit test of the parser with a fake git string covers (2), but completely misses (1) — git silently returns added+deleted when rename detection is disabled. REAL-INFRA: Git rename detection involves subprocess exit codes and flag-dependent output format; an in-process stub that returns a canned 'R\t...' string would always pass even if the invocation flags are wrong, hiding the real failure. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in the behavior disqualifies System; demoting to Integration would let a mocked subprocess mask missing -M flags.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository, commit a file, rename it with `git mv`, and commit again. Run the CLI tool targeting that repo. Assert: (a) the renamed file appears exactly once, in the 'renamed' category; (b) the 'added' and 'deleted' counts do not include the renamed file; (c) total category counts are correct.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The crash can originate from two places: (1) the parser encountering the literal 'Binary files ... differ' line git emits for binary diffs, or (2) the tool trying to decode or measure diff content for a binary file. A unit test with a canned string covers (1) only. REAL-INFRA: Real git produces subtly different binary diff markers depending on file type, git version, and whether --text is passed. An in-process stub would hardcode one format, hiding format variations that cause real crashes. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior says 'diffs that include binary files' — plural forms, real content — which requires real git to reproduce the exact output the parser must handle.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository, add a genuine binary file (e.g., a small PNG or a byte-sequence file with null bytes), commit it, then modify and re-commit it. Run the CLI tool. Assert: (a) the process exits with code 0 (no crash); (b) the binary file appears in the 'modified' count; (c) no exception traceback is present in stdout or stderr.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: An empty git repository has no HEAD ref. Running standard git commands (git log, git diff, git show) against it produces specific non-zero exit codes and stderr messages that are OS/subprocess phenomena. The failure mode — crash or error propagation — only manifests when the subprocess returns those exact error codes. MOCK-HIDE: A mocked subprocess would return whatever the test author programs; it cannot reproduce the actual error code git uses on a HEAD-less repo unless the author knows the exact failure in advance, which defeats the purpose of the test. REJECTION-GATE: Nothing in the behavior suggests the failure is in parsing logic alone; it is explicitly about what happens at invocation time on a real empty repo.
behaviors.test_description: Create a fresh temporary directory, run `git init` in it (do not make any commits). Run the CLI tool pointing at that directory. Assert: (a) the process exits with code 0; (b) stdout is a valid (empty) structured report with zero counts for all categories; (c) stderr contains no error or traceback.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: 'Actual change history' is definitionally the state of a real git object store. The correctness of counts depends on git correctly enumerating commits and the tool correctly parsing the full output. MOCK-HIDE: A mocked subprocess that returns a fixed diff listing would always yield the expected count, even if the tool incorrectly filters commits, passes wrong revision ranges, or miscounts merge commits — none of which would be detectable without a real repo with known ground truth. REJECTION-GATE: The word 'actual' in the behavior explicitly ties correctness to real git history, disqualifying any level that substitutes a fake for git.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository and perform a scripted sequence of changes: add 3 files, delete 2 files, modify 1 file, and rename 1 file, each as separate commits. Run the CLI tool. Assert: the report shows added=3, deleted=2, modified=1, renamed=1, with no overlap or double-counting between categories.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Git staging state (the index) is a real filesystem artifact managed by the git subprocess. Whether `git diff HEAD`, `git diff --cached`, or `git diff HEAD~1` is used, and how the tool switches between them, depends on real git index behavior. MOCK-HIDE: A stub subprocess cannot reproduce the distinction between staged and unstaged state — both calls would return the same canned response, making the test trivially pass regardless of whether the tool actually queries the index differently. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior specifically contrasts staged vs. unstaged, a real git state transition that has no meaningful in-process equivalent.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary git repository with an initial commit. Make a set of file changes (add, delete, modify). First run the CLI tool with changes unstaged and capture output-A. Then `git add` all changes and run the tool again, capturing output-B. Assert output-A equals output-B (same counts, same categories, same file list).
